text: This is a sixty-five-year-old female presenting with intermittent rectal bleeding. Patient also noticed a lump protruding through the anus when walking or during bowel movements. Workup was consistent with full thickness rectal prolapse. Patient also had concomitant constipation. Colonoscopy revealed mild diverticulosis.

The surgeon mobilizes the omentum and mesentery of the sigmoid and left colon for exposure. Using hook electrocautery, the mesentery along the left colon is further mobilized. The left ureter and gonadal vessels are identified and protected with a gentle sweeping motion posteriorly.

The surgeon exposes and identifies the inferior mesenteric artery, following and ligating the superior hemorrhoidal artery using hemoclips and ligature. Dissection of the rectosigmoid continues medially to laterally. The rectum is elevated anteriorly, releasing posterior attachments to the mesorectal fascia with hook electrocautery. The peritoneal layer along the anterior rectum is released, separating the rectum from the rectovaginal septum.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneum along the anterior rectum until it is fully mobilized from its attachment to the pelvic floor. The rectosigmoid is then transected using linear cutting staplers, and the specimen is removed through a fan and seal incision.

The surgeon re-identifies the ureter and precycle plexus before proceeding with the ligation of the sigmoid colon. The anvil is secured to the distal portion of the left colon at the extraction site, and bowel continuity is re-established using circular staples. The integrity of the anastomosis is confirmed visually with a water seal test and flexible sigmoidoscopy.
The surgeon performs a suture rectopexy, securing the rectum to the sacral promontory.
The surgeon measures the pouch of Douglas with a ruler. A biologic mesh, sized seven by three centimeters, is placed to obliterate the pouch of Douglas and is sutured securely in place.
Final pathology results showed no evidence of abnormality.
